topic: helping teen patient with college essay

turns.content: yá'át'ééh. so i have a question that is not exactly medical. i have this patient, she is 17, been seeing her since she was little. she asked me to help her with her college application essay and i said yes but now i'm looking at this blank page and thinking what did i get myself into
That's a great thing you're doing for her. College essays can be intimidating even for adults. What's the prompt she's working with, and does she have any ideas about what she wants to write about?

the prompt is something like describe a challenge you overcame and what it taught you. and she has plenty of challenges, you know. grew up out here, lost her dad young, her mom works two jobs. but she does not want to write a sad story. she told me that straight up. i respect that

I respect that too — and honestly, admissions readers see a lot of hardship essays. It can actually be more powerful to write about a challenge without making it a tragedy. What does she care about? What gets her excited or fired up? Sometimes the best essays come from a specific small moment, not the biggest thing that ever happened to you.

she is into biology. wants to be pre-med. she told me once that she started paying attention in science class because she wanted to understand her grandmother's diabetes. that is a real moment right there

That is a real moment — and it's exactly the kind of thing that works in an essay. It's personal, it connects to her goals, and it shows motivation that comes from her community, not just textbook interest. She could write about that specific moment: sitting with her grandmother, noticing something, asking a question, and how that question led her toward medicine. Does she remember the details of when that clicked for her?

i think so. she said her grandmother's feet were swollen and she asked why and nobody could explain it in a way that made sense. so she looked it up herself. she was maybe 13 or 14. you know that is the kind of kid she is. does not wait for answers, goes and finds them

That's the essay right there. A 13-year-old who sees her grandmother's swollen feet, doesn't get a satisfying answer, and goes to find out for herself — that's curiosity and initiative in one story. She should write it like she's telling you the story: what she saw, what she felt, what she did about it, and where it's taking her now. Admissions people want to see how you think, not just what happened to you.

okay that is good. now how long should this thing be. she said 650 words. that is not a lot. she tends to write long, you know. big paragraphs. i told her to cut it down but she looked at me like i told her to cut off her arm

Ha — 650 words is tight, but that's actually a good thing. It forces you to cut anything that doesn't earn its place. Here's what I'd suggest:

1. Write the first draft without worrying about length — just get the story out
2. Then cut ruthlessly. Every sentence should either move the story forward or reveal something about who she is
3. Start in the moment — don't waste words on setup like "When I was 13 years old, I was living with my grandmother." Just drop right in: her grandmother's feet, the question, the search for answers

Tell her the first draft is supposed to be too long. That's normal. The editing is where the real essay shows up.
that is practical advice. i will tell her that. the other thing is she asked me if she should mention being navajo. she is not sure if it sounds like she is playing a card, you know

It's not playing a card if it's her life. Being Navajo isn't a strategy — it's who she is, and it shapes everything about why she wants to go into medicine and who she wants to serve. She doesn't need to lead with it or make it the whole essay, but if her grandmother lives on the reservation and healthcare access is part of the story, then it's just the truth.

The key is to be specific, not general. Don't write "as a Native American I faced challenges." Write about her grandmother's feet and the 90-minute drive to the clinic. That's real. Admissions readers can tell the difference.

that is exactly right. be specific. i keep telling my patients the same thing — do not tell me you feel bad, tell me where it hurts. same idea. okay i feel better about helping her now. she is coming by saturday and we are going to sit down and work on it together

That's a perfect comparison — same skill, different context. When she comes Saturday, maybe start by just having her tell you the story out loud. Sometimes people write stiff but talk naturally. Record it on her phone or take notes, and then help her turn that into the draft. She already has the story. She just needs someone to help her see it.

ahéhee'. i like that idea, just have her talk first. she is a good kid. smart. works hard. she deserves a shot, you know. not a lot of kids from out here make it to college and i want her to be one who does

She's already got a lot going for her — curiosity, work ethic, and someone in her corner who cares enough to spend a Saturday on an essay. That matters more than people think. Good luck on Saturday, and tell her to trust her own story. It's a good one.
